← the Atlas

The active-tile indicator — a channel apart from agent status

feature · seedling ·proposed ·JUN 13, 2026

The focused-tile cue must live off the border (owned by repo identity + the animated agent-status ring) AND stay visible across every app-mode × terminal-theme combination — including a light-themed tile on the dark canvas, the case that broke the first three attempts. A wide menu of distinct mechanisms, each shown in both modes against a light active tile.

Contents

  • Today — and why it’s out
  • The options
    • A — Accent title bar
    • B — Lift / elevation (mode-aware)
    • C — Outer accent glow
    • D — Spotlight (dim the rest)
    • E — Canvas spotlight pool
    • F — Corner brackets (viewfinder)
    • G — Scale hierarchy
    • H — Accent top rail
  • How they sort

The canvas tile carries an animated agent-status ring on its border (marching ants / sweeping comet, in the terminal’s own theme colour — see canvas-tile-state-borders). That ring + the repo-identity border colour mean the border is spoken for. The active / focused tile must be marked on a completely separate channel — never another coloured border.

Two hard constraints (both learned the hard way)

  1. Off the border. Focus cannot be a ring, an edge accent, or anything that sits on the border the status comet travels — it reads as a second, competing border.
  2. Visible in every environment. App mode (dark / light) and terminal theme (dark / light) flip independently → four combinations. The cue must survive all four — most punishingly a light-themed tile (e.g. Gruvbox Material Light) on the dark canvas, which is where the lift’s black drop-shadow and then a faint white glow each disappeared.

The insight that should drive the choice

An absolute cue — a mark that must be legible on its own (a shadow, a glow, a strip in a fixed colour) — faces the four-combo contrast problem head-on and has to be made mode-aware (read the app mode) or theme-locked (derived from the tile) to survive. A relative cue — active simply differs from inactive (the others dim, desaturate, or shrink) — sidesteps the problem entirely, because within any single environment the two states only have to differ from each other, which they always do. The relative options below (D, G, and the “recede” half of B) are robust by construction.

Every tile wears its agent-status comet (the spinning border light). The middle tile is the focused one — and it is deliberately a light-themed terminal (Gruvbox-Material-Light-ish: cream bg, brown fg) so each option is judged in the hard case. Both rows are the same option: dark app mode on top, light app mode below.

Today — and why it’s out

rejected The focused tile got a teal accent on its right edge + a 1px accent ring — sitting on the same border the status comet travels, so it read as a second border fighting the first.

app dark mode
blog · codex
◐ working
argh · claude
⏵ waiting
odu · claude
◐ working

The teal edge + ring overlap the spinning comet — the exact clash that started this.

The options

A — Accent title bar

Focus lives in the chrome: the title bar tints toward the accent + gains a thin accent underline. Never touches the border. Shipped first ( #1335 ) — then pulled: the fixed accent tint clashes on themed tiles (it looked wrong on a light tile and on Iceberg), and a tint over a light terminal bg barely reads. An absolute cue with a theme-clash problem.

app dark mode
blog · codex
◐ working
argh · claude
⏵ waiting
odu · claude
◐ working
app light mode
blog · codex
◐ working
argh · claude
⏵ waiting
odu · claude
◐ working

A · accent title bar — clean separation, but a fixed accent on a themed/light tile clashes or washes out.

B — Lift / elevation (mode-aware)

The focused tile raises and the others recede (dim). The lift is now mode-aware: a dark drop-shadow on the light canvas, a bright glow on the dark canvas (a black shadow is invisible on near-black). The recede half is relative and always reads; the lift half is the absolute part that needed the mode-aware fix. Calmest and most physical.

app dark mode
blog · codex
◐ working
argh · claude
⏵ waiting
odu · claude
◐ working
app light mode
blog · codex
◐ working
argh · claude
⏵ waiting
odu · claude
◐ working

B · lift + recede — depth, no colour. The glow/shadow flips with app mode so it survives the dark canvas.

C — Outer accent glow

A tight accent halo hugging the tile edge. Different geometry from the on-border comet, but it still puts the accent colour right next to the border, so on a dense canvas it reads busy beside the comet — and the accent can clash with the terminal theme. An absolute, accent-coloured cue.

app dark mode

◐ working

◐ working
app light mode
blog · codex
◐ working
argh · claude
⏵ waiting
odu · claude
◐ working

C · outer accent glow — accent off the edge, but busy next to the comet and theme-clash prone.

D — Spotlight (dim the rest)

The inverse: don’t mark the active tile — instead dim + desaturate every inactive tile. The focused one is simply the only crisp, full-colour tile. Nothing is ever added to the border, and because it’s a relative cue it survives every mode × theme with no tuning. Strongest on a busy canvas; subtle when only one or two tiles exist.

app dark mode

◐ working

⏵ waiting

◐ working
app light mode
blog · codex
◐ working
argh · claude
⏵ waiting
odu · claude
◐ working

D · spotlight — inactive tiles fade to grey; the active one is the only one in colour. Robust by construction.

E — Canvas spotlight pool

A soft radial pool of light on the canvas behind the active tile — like a spotlight from above. It’s drawn on the canvas, whose colour is fixed by app mode alone, so it’s mode-aware (bright pool in dark mode, a dark pool in light mode) and completely independent of the tile’s theme. The tile sits in the light rather than wearing a mark.

app dark mode

app light mode
blog · codex
◐ working
argh · claude
⏵ waiting
odu · claude
◐ working

E · canvas pool — the focus cue lives on the canvas, never on the tile, so the terminal theme can’t break it.

F — Corner brackets (viewfinder)

Four accent L-brackets at the tile’s outer corners — a targeting reticle, not a continuous ring. Distinct geometry from the comet (corners only, and outside the edge), and because the brackets sit on the canvas they only need to contrast the (mode-known) canvas, not the theme. Crisp and unmistakable; slightly more “UI chrome” in feel.

app dark mode

⏵ waiting
odu · claude
◐ working

F · corner brackets — a viewfinder reticle outside the corners; reads as “targeted”, off the border, off the theme.

G — Scale hierarchy

The active tile pops larger (scale ~1.06); inactive tiles shrink + dim. A relative, physical size hierarchy — the focused tile is literally the biggest. Robust by construction (no colour, no absolute mark), but it nudges layout, so it’s best as a small scale paired with the recede.

app dark mode

argh · claude
⏵ waiting
odu · claude
◐ working

G · scale hierarchy — the active tile is the largest; the rest step back. Pure relative size.

H — Accent top rail

A minimal cousin of A: just a 3px accent rail along the title bar’s top edge (plus the accent dot), instead of tinting the whole bar. Lighter touch than A, so it clashes less, but it’s still an absolute accent mark and can fight a warm-themed tile.

app dark mode

argh · claude
⏵ waiting
odu · claude
◐ working

H · accent top rail — a thin accent strip on the chrome; A’s idea at lower volume.

How they sort

Absolute vs relative — pick the axis first

  • Relative (robust by construction): D spotlight · G scale · the recede half of B. They only need active ≠ inactive, so no combo can break them. D is the cleanest of these on a busy canvas.
  • Off-tile absolute (mode-aware, theme-independent): E canvas pool · F corner brackets. Drawn on the canvas, so they dodge the theme entirely and only need to track app mode — which we already know.
  • On-tile absolute (need care): A title-bar tint · C accent glow · H accent rail. All carry a fixed accent that can clash with the terminal theme; A and H also wash out on light tiles. B’s lift is on-tile but neutralised by going mode-aware.

Where to look first

If the cue should be impossible to break across themes, start with D (spotlight the active by dimming the rest) or E (canvas pool) — both sidestep the theme. If you want depth without colour, B (mode-aware lift) or G (scale). The accent-on-chrome family (A / H / C) is the most likely to clash and the reason the first attempt was pulled.

Status: proposed — reopened after A (accent title bar, #1335 ) failed the visibility/clash bar in live testing. A follow-on to the agent-status ring (canvas-tile-state-borders).
